hero.eyebrow: Medical Sales · Goldman Sachs Alum · Licensed EMT · DFW
hero.name: Kelsey
hero.tagline: Built across missions, boardrooms, and trauma bays.

narrative.title: How do you just make things happen?
narrative.body:
People have asked me my whole life how I just make things happen. I never had a good answer until recently.

As a Spanish speaking missionary across three states, I inherited the territory everyone before me had written off. I built it into our most productive account through one relationship that unlocked everything. A corporate career that took me from Vivint Solar, where I identified a $2M opportunity and sold it to senior leadership, to Goldman Sachs, where my hiring manager called my interview one of the best he had ever seen. Most recently, finishing my EMT program in the top 5% of my class and finding myself in emergency rooms and trauma bays executing under pressure alongside physicians.

Different rooms. Same instinct. Read the person. Understand what they need. Earn their trust. Find a way to close the gap. That is what I have been doing my entire life. In medical sales it just finally has a name.

startStories.title: The Room With No Margin for Error
startStories.teaser: At Goldman Sachs I managed contracts where a single misread detail could cost a relationship, and that pressure built the kind of executive presence that travels from the OR to the C suite.
startStories.body:
At Goldman Sachs I managed syndicated loan contracts and presented directly to institutional clients and internal stakeholders. The contracts ran hundreds of pages of dense legal language, and a single misread detail could cost the relationship real money and real trust.

I learned to read every contract front to back before I ever sat across from a client, so I could speak with the same confidence a senior decision maker expected from someone above my level. I anticipated their concerns before they raised them, translated complexity into plain terms, and held their trust through some of the most complex processes on the desk.

That built real executive presence, not the polished kind that only shows up in a boardroom, but the kind that holds steady with anyone from a scrub tech to a C suite executive. What that experience taught me is that presence isn't a personality trait, it's built one accurate, well prepared conversation at a time. That's exactly the discipline it takes to close deals in medical sales.

startStories.title: The Territory Strategy That Traveled
startStories.teaser: During an eighteen month volunteer assignment I inherited a territory everyone had given up on, and the strategy that turned it around is the same one I'm using to study Smart Care.
startStories.body:
During an 18 month volunteer representative role across Nebraska, Iowa, and South Dakota, our quota was defined by how many people we could convert into members of our organization. I inherited a territory that was underperforming, and the obvious approaches weren't working.

I turned it around through territory research, stakeholder mapping, active listening for the actual need behind every objection, and enough flexibility to change course when the direct path didn't work. Looking back, that's my Gallup Strategic strength showing up before I had a name for it.

That same instinct is how I approached Smart Care. I studied the platform's wearables and workflow tools, not because I had to, but because I found a product I genuinely believe in. I'd bring that same strategic approach to the team, and the same willingness to learn from coaching and feedback along the way, until I'm the best rep for my area.

startStories.title: The Moment No One Else Moved
startStories.teaser: During an EMT rotation a surgeon called for help mid emergency, and I was the only student who moved. That instinct is exactly how I approach a sales cycle.
startStories.body:
As an EMT finishing in the top 5% of my class, I trained in emergency rooms alongside physicians. During one rotation the surgeon was mid procedure when he needed extra hands and called out instructions to the three EMT students watching from the corner of the room.

The nurses were occupied. Everyone hesitated for a beat, except me. I moved immediately, before I had time to think it through, because the preparation was already there and the need was already clear.

That's my Activator strength in practice: not waiting for certainty, but acting the moment the need is obvious. I bring that same instinct to a sales cycle, real strategic preparation up front, followed by the willingness to act the second the moment calls for it.
